items.title: Glioblastoma: prognosis and management
items.contentHtml:
Glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade 4)

  • Prognosis: median survival of 12-15 months with standard treatment (surgery + radiotherapy + temozolomide).
  • Key marker: methylation of the O6-methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase promoter predicts better response to temozolomide.
  • Typical presentation: irregular ring enhancement with central necrosis on MRI.
  • Trap examination: not to be confused with brain abscess; restricted diffusion on MRI suggests abscess, not glioblastoma.
  • Age: peak in adults over 50 years; IDH mutation (isocitrate dehydrogenase) is rare in primary glioblastoma (90% are IDH wild-type).

items.title: Infantile medulloblastoma
items.contentHtml:
Medulloblastoma in posterior fossa

  • Location: cerebellar vermis, fourth ventricle; It is the most common malignant brain tumor in children.
  • Clinic: intracranial hypertension syndrome (morning headache, projectile vomiting), truncal ataxia and conjugate gaze palsy.
  • Image: hyperdense solid tumor in computed tomography, with homogeneous enhancement and restriction in diffusion.
  • Prognosis: molecular classification (WNT, SHH, group 3 and 4) determines risk; WNT has a better prognosis (survival >90%).
  • Treatment: maximum resection + craniospinal radiotherapy + chemotherapy (avoid radiotherapy in children under 3 years of age due to toxicity).

items.title: Meningioma: benign and origin
items.contentHtml:
Meningioma

  • Origin: arachnoid cells (meningeal coverings); most are benign (WHO grade 1).
  • Epidemiology: more common in women (2:1), peak in 40-70 years; associated with neurofibromatosis type 2.
  • Image: extra-axial tumor, broad dural base, homogeneous enhancement and sometimes calcification; dural tail sign.
  • Frequent location: cerebral convexity, wings of the sphenoid, olfactory and parasagittal groove.
  • Examination trap: atypical (grade 2) or anaplastic (grade 3) meningiomas have a higher recurrence rate; indicate adjuvant radiotherapy if resection is incomplete.

items.title: Brain metastases
items.contentHtml:
Brain metastases

  • Most frequent origin: lung cancer (40-50%), followed by breast, melanoma, colon and kidney.
  • Location: in the cortico-subcortical junction of the gray-white matter, especially in territories of the middle cerebral artery.
  • Presentation: headache, seizures, focal deficit; multiple lesions on MRI with ring enhancement and perilesional vasogenic edema.
  • Treatment: dexamethasone for edema, holocranial radiotherapy, stereotactic radiosurgery for ≤3 lesions and surgery if a single accessible lesion.
  • Trap examination: melanoma metastasis may be hyperintense on T1 without melanin contrast.

items.title: intracranial hypertension
items.contentHtml:
Intracranial hypertension syndrome

  • Definition: sustained intracranial pressure >20 mmHg (>25 cmH2O in lumbar puncture).
  • Clinic: morning headache, projectile vomiting without nausea, bilateral papilledema, sixth nerve palsy (false localization).
  • Late signs: bradycardia, arterial hypertension and irregular breathing (Cushing's triad).
  • Tumor causes: tumor mass effect, perilesional edema, obstruction of cerebrospinal fluid (hydrocephalus).
  • Acute management: dexamethasone 10 mg intravenous bolus, then 4-6 mg every 6 hours; General measures: head at 30°, avoid hypotension.

items.title: Initial seizures
items.contentHtml:
Convulsive seizures as tumor debut

  • Frequency: 30-50% of brain tumors present with seizures; more common in supratentorial tumors (astrocytomas, oligodendrogliomas).
  • Type: focal (motor or sensory) or secondary generalized seizures; in low-grade tumors, seizures may be the only symptom for years.
  • Mechanism: cortical irritation by the tumor and perilesional edema; imbalance between glutamate and GABA.
  • First-line treatment: levetiracetam (500-1500 mg twice a day) due to less interaction with chemotherapy; avoid phenytoin if radiotherapy is used (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
  • Examination trap: anticonvulsant prophylaxis is not indicated in patients without seizures; Just treat if it appears.

items.title: Supratentorial vs infratentorial
items.contentHtml:
Supratentorial vs. infratentorial location

  • Supratentorial: tumors of the cerebral hemispheres (gliomas, meningiomas, metastases); clinical features: seizure, motor/sensory deficit, aphasia.
  • Infratentorial: cerebellum and brainstem (medulloblastoma, pilocytic astrocytoma, ependymoma); Clinical features: ataxia, vertigo, cranial nerve palsy, obstructive hydrocephalus.
  • In adults: supratentorial tumors predominate (70%); in children, infratentorial ones are more frequent (60%).
  • Herniation: supratentorial → subfalcial or uncal herniation; infratentorial → tonsillar herniation (risk of respiratory arrest).
  • Image: MRI with contrast defines the exact location and relationship with neighboring structures.

items.title: Pilocytic astrocytoma
items.contentHtml:
Pilocytic astrocytoma (WHO grade 1)

  • Epidemiology: most common tumor in children and adolescents; typical location in the cerebellum (posterior fossa), also in the optic nerve and chiasm.
  • Image: mural nodule with enhancement and large cyst (target image); does not enhance the cystic wall.
  • Genetics: BRAF-KIAA1549 gene fusion in most; does not usually have IDH mutation.
  • Prognosis: excellent; Complete resection is curative in >90% of cases; rare recurrence if total resection.
  • Trap examination: not to be confused with hemangioblastoma (mural nodule with cyst, but in adults and with association with von Hippel-Lindau disease).

items.title: Acoustic neuroma
items.contentHtml:
Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)

  • Origin: Schwann cells of the vestibular branch of the eighth cranial nerve; benign (WHO grade 1).
  • Clinical: progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo; large tumor can compress the brain stem and cause hydrocephalus.
  • Image: tumor in cerebellopontine angle, with homogeneous enhancement and extension to the internal auditory canal (funnel sign).
  • Association: neurofibromatosis type 2 if bilateral (diagnostic criterion).
  • Treatment: surveillance if small and asymptomatic; stereotactic radiosurgery or surgery if growth or symptoms.

items.title: Craniopharyngioma
items.contentHtml:
Suprasellar craniopharyngioma

  • Origin: remains of the epithelium of Rathke's pouch; benign tumor but with a tendency to recur.
  • Location: suprasellar (more common) or intrasellar; produces chiasmatic compression (bitemporal hemianopsia) and endocrinopathies (panhypopituitarism, diabetes insipidus).
  • Image: cystic tumor with calcifications (in computed tomography) and ring enhancement of the solid component; Hyperintense cystic content on T1 due to proteinaceous content.
  • Peak age: bimodal: children (5-15 years) and adults (50-70 years).
  • Treatment: complete surgical resection if possible; Adjuvant radiotherapy in subtotal resection.

items.foco: Primary CNS lymphoma in immunosuppressed patients
items.title: Primary CNS lymphoma
items.contentHtml:
Primary lymphoma of the central nervous system

  • Epidemiology: associated with immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS, transplant, immunosuppressive therapy); in HIV, it is related to Epstein-Barr virus.
  • Image: periventricular lesions, with homogeneous enhancement and restricted diffusion; in immunocompetent patients it may be single, in immunosuppressed patients it may be multifocal.
  • Diagnosis: stereotactic biopsy; previous dexamethasone can disappear the lesion (lymphocytolytic effect), making the diagnosis difficult.
  • Treatment: chemotherapy with high-dose methotrexate (3-8 g/m²) plus rituximab; Holocranial radiotherapy is reserved for neurotoxicity.
  • Examination trap: do not use corticosteroids before biopsy if lymphoma is suspected, as it can mask the diagnosis.

items.title: Dexamethasone and cerebral edema
items.contentHtml:
Dexamethasone in vasogenic cerebral edema

  • Mechanism: reduces the permeability of the blood-brain barrier, reducing peritumoral edema; effect in 12-24 hours.
  • Loading dose: 10 mg intravenous bolus; then 4-6 mg every 6 hours (total dose 16-24 mg/day).
  • Indication: symptomatic edema due to primary tumors or metastases; also in radionecrosis.
  • Adverse effects: hyperglycemia, proximal myopathy, insomnia, osteoporosis, immunosuppression; do not use prophylactically in all patients.
  • Examination trap: dexamethasone is the corticosteroid of choice due to its low mineralocorticoid activity; Do not use prednisone (less penetration into the central nervous system).
